nodes.text: Cold today. You feel that coming in? Got that damp edge to it. Feels like it wants to snow but can't quite commit.

responses.text: It does. Been like that all week.
What can I help you with today?

nodes.text: That's Ohio for you. Used to be worse when I was on the route. Carrying mail in that kind of weather — bag gets heavy. You don't want to know what a wet catalog does to your shoulder after twenty years.

responses.text: Mail carrier? How long were you on the route?

nodes.text: Monthly pickup. Carl Dunbar. The warfarin. I know the drill — I'll need my ID scanned, you'll ask me two or three things, then I'll grab a paper and be out of your way.

responses.text: Sounds right. Let me see your ID.

nodes.text: You know it. Carl Dunbar. Fifteen years in this Tuesday slot — give or take. They know my name here. At least they used to. You don't, of course, but I'm not holding it against you.

responses.text: Fifteen years is impressive. Let me pull you up.

nodes.text: Twenty-three years. Same neighborhood the whole time. I knew every dog on that route by name. The dogs were better than the people about being on time, honestly. *chuckles* They've got kids on those routes now running around with carts. Kids don't know what they're doing.

responses.text: Different era. Can I see your ID?

nodes.text: Here you go. I'll grab a paper while you pull me up. They still have the TV Guide, or did they stop carrying it?

responses.text: We've got both over by the register.

nodes.text: Good. Some things should stay the same.
